original_hypothesis.title: Unbounded gauge creation allows Block Gas Limit DoS on emissions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The `distributeAll()` and `distributeFees()` functions in `GaugeManager.sol` iterate over the entire `pools` array. Anyone can call `createGauge()` to add new whitelisted pools to this array. An attacker can create hundreds of dust pools with whitelisted tokens and deploy gauges for them, bloating the `pools` array until iterating over it exceeds the block gas limit, permanently halting protocol emissions and fee distributions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: While `distribute(uint256,uint256)` exists for partial distribution, the automated `EpochController.performUpkeep()` explicitly calls the unbounded `distributeAll()` and `distributeFees()`. If these revert, the epoch cannot flip automatically.
preconditions: Attacker has enough capital to deploy dust pools and gauges
Tokens used are whitelisted and connectors

initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack or extortion to stop DoS

economic_questions: How much gas does one iteration of _distribute cost?
How many gauges are needed to hit the Avalanche block gas limit (8M or 15M)?
validation_requirements: Calculate cost of deploying N gauges
Test performUpkeep with bloated pools array

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of deploying ~100-500 pairs and gauges (gas costs)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Block gas limit vs loop iteration cost

validation_result.mechanism: Unbounded loop DoS on automated epoch transition
validation_result.impact_summary: The automated epoch transition via EpochController.performUpkeep() calls distributeAll() and distributeFees(), both of which iterate over the entire unbounded pools array. Permissionless gauge creation allows this array to grow until these functions exceed the block gas limit, permanently halting automated emissions and fee distribution.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability exists in the interaction between three components: (1) GaugeManager._createGauge() is permissionless and appends to the pools array with no removal mechanism, (2) GaugeManager.distributeAll() and distributeFees() iterate over the entire pools array in unbounded for-loops, and (3) EpochController.performUpkeep() exclusively calls these unbounded versions rather than the ranged alternatives distribute(start, finish) and distributeFees(start, finish). While createGauge requires whitelisted tokens and connector tokens, for CL pools (gaugeType == 1) the isPair check is bypassed (set to true directly). With multiple custom pool deployers, an attacker can create many distinct pool addresses for the same token pair with different tick spacings. Each unique pool address allows a separate gauge creation, growing the pools array. The pools array is append-only with no mechanism to remove entries. Even without an attacker, natural protocol growth combined with no pool removal will eventually cause this issue. The Avalanche C-Chain block gas limit (8M-15M as shown in config) provides a hard ceiling on how many iterations are feasible.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code clearly shows: (1) permissionless createGauge at GaugeManager.sol, (2) append-only pools array in _saveBribeData, (3) unbounded loops in distributeAll() and distributeFees(), and (4) EpochController.performUpkeep() calling only the unbounded versions. The ranged alternatives exist but are not used by the automation system.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Unbounded loop over entire pools array. Each iteration involves multiple SLOADs and external calls (_updateForAfterDistribution, IVoter.weights, potentially gauge.notifyRewardAmount). Gas cost grows linearly with pools.length.
Unbounded loop over entire pools array. For CL gauges, each iteration involves multiple external calls to Algebra contracts (communityVault.withdraw, gauge.claimFees).

The automated epoch transition exclusively calls the unbounded distributeAll() and distributeFees() functions. It does not use the ranged distribute(start, finish) or distributeFees(start, finish) alternatives.
No access control beyond nonReentrant. Anyone can create gauges, which appends to the pools array via _saveBribeData.
Append-only pools array with no removal mechanism. The array grows monotonically with each gauge creation.

calling_sequence: 1. Chainlink Automation or EPOCH_MANAGER calls EpochController.performUpkeep()
2. performUpkeep calls checkUpkeep which calls IMinter(minter).check()
6. distributeAll() loops: for (x; x < pools.length; x++) { _distribute(gauges[pools[x]]); }

7. Each _distribute() call: reads gaugesDistributionTimestmap, calls _updateForAfterDistribution (which calls IVoter.weights), potentially calls gauge.notifyRewardAmount + IAlgebraEternalFarming.setRates
9. distributeFees() loops: for (i; i < pools.length; i++) { _distributeFees(pools[i]); }
10. Each _distributeFees() for CL: reads communityVault balances, calls withdraw(), calls gauge.claimFees()
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The performUpkeep function calls distributeAll() and distributeFees() sequentially, both iterating over the entire pools[] array. With pools[] growing unboundedly through permissionless createGauge(), the combined gas cost of both loops will eventually exceed the block gas limit (8M on Avalanche mainnet, 15M on Fuji testnet per hardhat config). Each iteration of _distribute involves at minimum 3-4 SLOADs plus an external call to IVoter.weights(), and each _distributeFees iteration for CL pools involves multiple external calls. At approximately 50-100k gas per iteration across both loops, the limit would be reached at roughly 80-300 pools depending on gauge types.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Block gas limit on target chain
config_validation.deployment_evidence: hardhat.config.js shows Avalanche mainnet gas: 8000000, Fuji gas: 8000000 / gasLimit: 15000000. deployment-flows/scripts/flip-epoch.js uses gasLimit: 15000000 for performUpkeep. No deployment scripts show any access control being added to createGauge.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: (1) Attacker identifies whitelisted token pairs. For CL pools (gaugeType=1), isPair is set to true without factory validation. (2) Attacker uses custom pool deployers to create many unique Algebra CL pools with same whitelisted tokens but different deployers/tick spacings. (3) For each unique pool address, attacker calls createGauge() to grow pools array. (4) Once pools array is large enough that distributeAll() + distributeFees() exceeds block gas limit, performUpkeep permanently reverts. (5) Automated epoch transitions halt. Manual intervention requires using the ranged distribute(start,finish) and distributeFees(start,finish) functions, but the EpochController automation cannot do this without contract upgrade. Even without a malicious attacker, natural protocol growth with no pool removal mechanism means this will eventually occur.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. Once the pools array grows beyond the gas limit threshold: (1) EpochController.performUpkeep() permanently reverts, halting automated epoch transitions. (2) Emission distribution and fee distribution cannot happen through the normal automated path. (3) Manual intervention requires calling the ranged distribute/distributeFees functions directly, bypassing the automation system. (4) If the epoch flip is delayed significantly, emissions scheduling is disrupted. (5) Protocol governance must either upgrade the EpochController to use ranged calls or manually manage distributions, which degrades the decentralized automation guarantee.
recommended_mitigations: Modify EpochController.performUpkeep() to use the ranged distribute(start, finish) and distributeFees(start, finish) functions with configurable batch sizes instead of distributeAll() and distributeFees()
Add a maximum pool count or implement access control (e.g., GAUGE_ADMIN role) on createGauge to prevent permissionless array bloat
Implement a mechanism to remove dead/killed gauges from the pools array to allow the array to shrink
Add a configurable batch size parameter to EpochController that splits distribution across multiple performUpkeep calls
Consider adding a removePool function that can be called by governance to compact the pools array
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts in a Hardhat/Foundry test environment. 2. Whitelist 2-3 test tokens as connectors. 3. Create CL pools via custom deployers with different tick spacings for the same token pairs. 4. Call createGauge() in a loop for each unique pool until pools.length reaches ~200-500. 5. Measure gas for distributeAll() and distributeFees() at various pool counts. 6. Identify the exact threshold where performUpkeep reverts due to out-of-gas. 7. Verify that the ranged alternatives distribute(start, finish) still work.
uncertainties: Exact gas cost per iteration of _distribute and _distributeFees depends on gauge type (CL vs V2) and state (alive vs killed, claimable > 0 vs 0)
The number of unique CL pools an attacker can create depends on the number of custom pool deployers and whitelisted token pairs
Avalanche C-Chain actual block gas limit may differ from the hardhat configuration values
The ITokenHandler whitelist and connector requirements limit the attack surface but don't prevent it
additional_testing_needed: Gas profiling: measure exact gas cost per iteration of _distribute() and _distributeFees() for both CL and V2 gauges
Determine the actual Avalanche C-Chain block gas limit (currently 15M)
Count available whitelisted token pairs and custom pool deployers to estimate maximum attacker-created pools
Test performUpkeep with incrementally larger pools arrays to find exact failure threshold
